speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11th, 1995. The survivor is David-- David Jakubowski, and this is tape number six.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are two letters tattooed on my wrist-- K-- capital K, capital L-- Konzentrationslager in German. In English, it would be concentration camps. And they done-- they tattooed me in-- in the year 1944, in the Camp Mielec. They just-- they tattooed every Jew because one of the inmates escaped with a Polish group from the factory. So they tattooed everyone with the two letters on the wrist. And the wrist had to be shown before leaving the factory to the guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Jakubowski, please introduce the lovely woman on your left.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Helen Jakubowski. I met her first in the labor camp in Lublin. And-- in Budzyn. Budzyn. And then she went somewhere else. We met after the war in 1946 in Hamburg. We got married in Hamburg on August 11th, 1946. So next year, August 11th, we'll celebrate our 50th anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Jakubowski, would you like to say a few words at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few words about what? About anything? Well, the-- my husband forgot that-- yeah. He sent somebody to-- I was liberated in Bergen-Belsen. And he found-- one of his patient's son or nephew went to look for his mother. And he said, in case you find a girl by the name of Helen first, tell her that here I am. It didn't take me too  long. He found me. I was his mother's friend. We both were liberated there-- or his aunts, whatever. And of course, I picked myself up and went to meet him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to Hamburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '45. But then I also-- I went to Poland to look for my family first, and then I came back [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for joining us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was me and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side is me, when I was 18 years old. And on the left is my sister, Ruth, when she was 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Poland, in Aleksandrow. This is after I finished the high school, taken in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And down below? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, taken after the war in Germany. I was in early 30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the Warsaw Ghetto. It was divided in two parts-- the large ghetto on the top, and the small ghetto down below it. Both the large-- the large and the small are connected by a bridge on-- over the [? Kludno ?] Street. We could walk from one ghetto to another, only to this bridge. So we had to walk up and then go down, because beneath the bridge was only for the Poles to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the bridge was over what street?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kludno. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Poles could walk on that street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the street-- under the bridge. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Now, what you see in front, on the top, is the train ticket, which I received in the POW hospital while-- after I was released from there on June 11th, 1940. And as you see, it's to Radom. My aunt lives there, the sister of my mother. On the bottom, is the back of the ticket. But I never went to Radom. I stopped in a Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the bottom is the other side of the ticket. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The statement which you see here in front of you is a statement from two German physicians from the POW camp where I worked in-- in beginning of 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in [INAUDIBLE], Poland, near Poznan. It was a small town. Poznan was a big city. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] What you see in front of you was given to me by the Judenrat on July 21st, 1942. It's in German, with my photograph on it, written in German to show it to the German soldiers on my way to Umschlagplatz. They said there that I am supposed to go to the Umschlagplatz, and I am taken out from the resettlement.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The document which you see in front of you was given by me-- to me on July 21st, 1942, by the Judenrat in Warsaw. I had to show it to the it German soldiers. It tells there that I am on my way to the Umschlagplatz and I am taken out from the resettlement.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's my picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what you see there is my picture. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This what you see in front of you is a document, but in Polish, given to me by the Judenrat, which I was supposed to show on my way to the Umschlagplatz, to the Jewish police, who understood Polish and didn't understand German. So I had a free road to the Umschlagplatz, and I was taken out from that resettlement. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] These are two documents. The top document is-- was given to me by the American forces. And it says that I was an inmate of the Flossenburg concentration camp, with the number 15076. [PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS] The lower document is a statement given to me just stating that I was liberated by the American Army on April 23rd, 1945, and the commanding officer of the battalion was Major Robertson. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is a picture of my son, Stephen Reginald Jakubowski. It was taken in 1981, when he was entering the law school in Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture of my daughter, Ruth Ann Jakubowski Gruen. It was taken-- this is a wedding picture, and it was taken in October, 1983 in Baltimore. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture is of myself in the middle, and my two grandsons, Benjamin on the right and Kenneth of the-- on the left. They are the sons of my daughter, Ruth. It was taken in 1994 in Baltimore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my daughter, Ruth Ann Jakubowski Gruen. And to the right is her son Benjamin A. Gruen. On the left, is the second son, Kenneth M. Gruen. In the middle, is the son of my son, Steven Jakubowski. The little boy's name is Alexander Michael Jakubowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken this year in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Baltimore?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Baltimore. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my grandson, Alexander Michael Jakubowski, the son of my son, Steven Reginald Jakubowski, in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken this year, in 1995.
